pattern: Turning the whole array into ones is only possible if a 1 can ever be produced at all, which is exactly when the array's overall gcd is 1 — after that, the cheapest path is to manufacture one 1 as fast as possible and then spread it outward.
hints: If a 1 is already present, every other element needs exactly one operation against a neighboring 1 to become 1 itself, so the answer is simply the count of elements that are not already 1.
If no 1 exists, first check whether it's even reachable: take the gcd of every element in the array — if that overall gcd isn't 1, no sequence of operations can ever create one.

Otherwise find the shortest contiguous run of elements whose combined gcd is 1; building that first 1 costs (run length minus one) operations, after which spreading it to every other position costs one operation per remaining element.

edge_inputs.description: a 1 already present alongside other values
no ones, but the whole array's gcd is already 1
smallest array, impossible since both share a common factor
shortest qualifying window sits right at the start
no pair reaches gcd 1 but the full array does

stress.description: random positive integers; overall gcd may or may not be 1, exercising both branches
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, min(size % 50 + 2, 50))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 1000000) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]
stress.note: unconstrained random values naturally cover both the possible and the impossible (-1) case across runs
